coredata.dc:description: Therapeutic modalities and drug formulations play a crucial and prominent role in actualizing effective treatment and radical cures of tumors. However, the therapeutic efficiency was severely limited by tumor recurrence and complex multi-step preparation of formulation. Therefore, the exploration of novel nanoparticles via a simple and green synthesis process for conquering traditional obstacles and improving therapeutic efficiency is an appealing, yet remarkably challenging task. Herein, a universal nanoplatform allows all cancerous cell-targeting, acid-responsive, cell imaging, synergistic chemotherapy, and nucleolar targeted phototherapy function was tactfully designed and constructed by using chemotherapeutic agents ursolic acid (UA), sorafenib (SF), and carbon dots (CDs) photosensitizers (PSs). The designed US NPs were formed by self-assembly of UA and SF associated with electrostatic, π-π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions. After hydrogen bonding reaction with CDs, the obtained (denoted as USC NPs) have a relatively uniform size of an average 125.6 nm, which facilitated the favorable accumulation of drugs at the tumor region through a potential enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect as compared to their counterpart of free CDs solution. Both in vitro and in vivo studies revealed that the advanced platform commenced synergistic anticancer therapeutic potency, imperceptible systematical toxicity, and remarkable reticence towards drug-resistant cancer cells. Moreover, the CDs PSs possess intrinsic nucleolus-targeting ability. Taken together, this theranostics system can fully play the role of “killing three birds with one stone” in a safe manner, implying a promising direction for exploring treatment strategies for cancer and endowing them with great potential for future translational research and providing a new vision for the advancing of an exceptionally forceful protocol for practical cancer therapy.

head.ce:title: Rational design of a minimum nanoplatform for maximizing therapeutic potency: Three birds with one stone

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Therapeutic modalities and drug formulations play a crucial and prominent role in actualizing effective treatment and radical cures of tumors. However, the therapeutic efficiency was severely limited by tumor recurrence and complex multi-step preparation of formulation. Therefore, the exploration of novel nanoparticles via a simple and green synthesis process for conquering traditional obstacles and improving therapeutic efficiency is an appealing, yet remarkably challenging task. Herein, a universal nanoplatform allows all cancerous cell-targeting, acid-responsive, cell imaging, synergistic chemotherapy, and nucleolar targeted phototherapy function was tactfully designed and constructed by using chemotherapeutic agents ursolic acid (UA), sorafenib (SF), and carbon dots (CDs) photosensitizers (PSs). The designed US NPs were formed by self-assembly of UA and SF associated with electrostatic, π-π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions. After hydrogen bonding reaction with CDs, the obtained (denoted as USC NPs) have a relatively uniform size of an average 125.6 nm, which facilitated the favorable accumulation of drugs at the tumor region through a potential enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect as compared to their counterpart of free CDs solution. Both in vitro and in vivo studies revealed that the advanced platform commenced synergistic anticancer therapeutic potency, imperceptible systematical toxicity, and remarkable reticence towards drug-resistant cancer cells. Moreover, the CDs PSs possess intrinsic nucleolus-targeting ability. Taken together, this theranostics system can fully play the role of “killing three birds with one stone” in a safe manner, implying a promising direction for exploring treatment strategies for cancer and endowing them with great potential for future translational research and providing a new vision for the advancing of an exceptionally forceful protocol for practical cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has already become one of the uppermost causes of death internationally and is currently a serious menace to the health of human beings and the quality of life [1]. It continues a decisive threat to dispose of cancers without any recurrence accompanying a single treatment course [2]. Particularly, most tumors cannot be eliminated or yet developed slowly with mono-therapeutic modalities, such as radiotherapy or chemotherapy only. In addition, multidrug resistance (MDR) is also committed to the failure of therapeutics in each restorative cycle. This may be due to the drug discharge of tumor cells, which broadly reduces the performance of chemotherapy [3]. Decades of experiments and clinical efforts have exhibited that the combination treatment was regarded as a more adopted remedial method for cancer treatment [4–6]. However, a few necessitated people were, even so, terrorized to death as a result of the tumor recurrence despite they were having taken remedial measures that only transitorily constrained tumor expansion [7]. Hence, it has become a formidable hurdle to handling a combination treatment that can conquer the drug intransigence and eliminate the tumor during treatment courses without recrudescence.

Benefiting from the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have become the most explored objects for constructing decisive therapeutic platforms [8–10]. Among them, CDs have emerged as a kind of promising candidate for realizing cancer diagnosis and treatment simultaneously thanks to their superior biocompatibility, stable fluorescence (FL) emission, facile surface functionalization, and more importantly, the potential photo-mediated therapeutic functions, such as photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), that can destroy tumor cells through cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) or local hyperthermia converted from irradiation light [11–13]. Even more, PDT could eliminate tumor drug resistance and the associated tumor recurrence assumably because of its direct injurious to proteins that are in charge of the drug-resistant [14]. Thereinto, numerous CDs have been synthesized through design unequal antecedent and implemented a broaden applications, such as sensing, biosensing, and nanomedicine [10,15]. Although extensive research has been conducted for the designing and fabricating of CDs-based theranostic systems, the small size character of CDs (2–10 nm) limits their efficient accumulation in tumor sites due to the poor EPR effect, consequently reducing theranostic outcomes [16,17]. Thus, the development of a simple method to fabricate nanoplatforms with satisfactory outcomes is of vital significance yet continues a strenuous burden with the very finite success attained.

Among all the traditional therapeutic modalities, the fashion in clinical exploration has constantly shifted from focusing on single therapy to combination therapy. More importantly, the cooperative enhancement interactions between several types of monotherapy contribute to the naissance of multimodal synergistic therapy, leading to remarkable superadditive (namely “1 + 1 greater than 2”) effects, more vigorous than any single therapy or their theoretical combination [18,19]. Of note, SF, a Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved clinical drug that curbs tumor cell proliferation and induces apoptosis of cancer cells [20]. Insufficiently, the SF suffers from MDR, and thus research for drug sensitizers has become an excessive challenge [21]. UA, which can execute molecular targets of diverse gesturing pathways, impedes the expansion of carcinoma cells and thereby exerts anticancer activity [22,23]. Most importantly, in addition to increasing the sensitivity of cancer cells to SF, UA also has the effect of alleviating cancer cell resistance. Therefore, the combination of UA and SF to enhance the sensitivity of cancer cells to SF and destroy its toxicity has become a positive therapeutic strategy [22,24].

Citric acid (99.5 %), ethylenediamine (98 %), Ursolic acid (UA) (98 %), sorafenib (SF) (98 %), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) (98 %), and 3-diphenyliso-benzofuran (DPBF) (97 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). In vitro ROS probe [Dichlorodihydro fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA)] was purchased from meilunbio. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Hoechst 33,342 (2′-[4-ethoxyphenyl]-5-[4-methyl-1-piperazinyl]-2,5′-bi-1H-benzimidazole trihydrochloride trihydrate) was obtained from ThermFisher Scientific (Shanghai, China). MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5‑diphenyl tetrazolium bromide] was obtained from Life Technologies GMbH (Darmstadt, Germany). Cy5.5 was obtained from Lumiprobe Corporation (Hunt Valley, ML, USA). HepG2 cells, SMMC 7721 cells, and L02 cells were purchased from the Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). All cell-culture-related reagents were obtained from Life Technologies GMbH (Darmstadt, Germany) unless stated otherwise.

The citric acid (2 g) was added to the ethylenediamine (3 mL) under magnetic stimulation. The solution was then transmitted into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (20 mL) and subjected to hydrothermal treatment in an air stove at 180 ± 2 ℃ for 6 h, thereupon chilled to room temperature naturally after the reaction. The obtained CDs were dark brown and centrifugated at 10000 rpm for 15 min, followed by dialysis 14 000 MW cutoff, Sigma) for 48 h. Finally, the resultant was then lyophilized for subsequent use.

The US NPs was synthesized by the self-assembly method from free UA and SF. Briefly, 50 μL of UA aqueous solution (4 mg/mL) was added dropwise into the 50 μL of SF aqueous solution (4 mg/mL) through the vortex, then diluted with 900 μL of ultrapure water via vortex to form the product US NPs. Other formulations with different mass proportion of UA to SF were formulated consistently. The size and zeta potential of US NPs can be finely regulated by altering some specifications, for example, the consistency of drug solutions and the relative proportions of UA to SF.

The USC NPs were obtained through the same procedure as the US NPs. In concisely, 100 μL of US NPs (0.2 mg/mL) were annexed dropwise into the 100 μL of CDs solution through a vortex (mass ratio: US NPs/CDs = 1:3). The USC NPs were ultimately acquired and acutely utilized for the characteristics. Other formulas with distinctive mass proportions of US NPs to CDs were also formulated by adopting this method. Correspondingly, the size and zeta potential of the USC NPs can be adjusted by altering some parameters such as the mass ratio, concentration of drug solution, and the solvent used.

SEM images were characterized by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SUPRA 55, Carl Zeiss). TEM measurements were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (Talos F200i, USA) with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry spectra were recorded in the spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 by using an FT-IR 5700 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) and samples (100 mg) were freeze-dried at −40 °C in a vacuum for 12 h before making pellets with KBr powder. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra were conducted on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo Scientific) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) under ultrahigh vacuum. The infrared thermographic maps and regional maximum temperatures were obtained by using an infrared thermal imaging camera (Fortic 323pro, China). The cell image was obtained by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). The zeta potential and the size distribution were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy was analyzed by LAMBDA 950 spectrophotometer (USA). A fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer, France) was exploited to assess the fluorescence and quantum yield (QY) of free CDs and USC NPs in water in a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. Encapsulation efficiency (EE) of free drugs in the filtrate was calculated based on Eq (1):
(1)
EE ( % ) = ( t o t a l d r u g - f r e e d r u g ) / t o t a l d r u g × 100

The QY measurements of CDs were performed regarding established procedures in the literature [25]. Quinine sulfate (QY was 54 % under 360 nm excitation in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution) was selected as the standard. The calculation reference formula of CDs refers to Equation (2):
(2)

Where ψ, I, A, and η represent QY, fluorescence emission intensity, absorption under excitation wavelength irradiation, and the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. While the subscript r stands for the quinine sulfate solution (standard substance).

The singlet oxygen generation of CDs and USC NPs were detected with DPBF as a ROS fluorescent probe. Briefly, 2.7 mg DPBF was dissolved in 1 mL DMSO to obtain the resulting DPBF solution (10 mM), subsequently, the DPBF solution was added to the sample (50 μg/mL CDs and USC NPs at an equivalent concentration of CDs) with absorbance around 1.5. Then the mixture was irradiated using a 638 nm laser irradiation at 500 mW/cm2 for 10 min. The oxidation of DPBF (at 415 nm) vs irradiation time was monitored by a UV–vis spectrophotometer every minute.

Following Shi's [26] report, a thermal infrared image was employed for estimating the photothermal performance of CDs and USC NPs. Firstly, CDs (100 μg/mL) and USC NPs at an equivalent concentration of CDs were placed into disposable cuvettes and exposed to laser irradiation of 638 nm at different power densities (0.13–2.53 W/cm2). Then, various concentrations (0–200 μg/mL) of CDs and USC NPs aqueous solutions were loaded into disposable cuvettes and irradiated by a laser of 638 nm at fixed power density (1 W/cm2). Finally, photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was determined according to the following equation:

Relative parameters are defined as follows: h is the heat transfer coefficient; A represents the surface area of the cuvette; ΔTmax, mix and ΔTmax, waters are the maximum temperature change of USC NPs and deionized water during the irradiation process, respectively. I represent used laser power, and Aλ is the absorbance of USC NPs at 638 nm. η is the conversion efficiency from incident laser energy to thermal energy.

Stability is one of the crucial factors for the biomedical applications of a nano-scaled drug delivery system. The stability study of US NPs (UA/SF = 1:1, mass ratio) and USC NPs (US NPs/CDs = 1:3, mass ratio) stored at room temperature and dispersed in ultra-pure water were investigated by measuring the size and zeta potential in the mixed solution daily for up to five days.

The drug release curves were obtained with the dialysis method [27]. All sampling was packed in dialysis bags (molecular weight cut-off: 3500 Da) and soaked in PBS buffer (pH 5.5 or 7.4). The pipes were retained at room temperature in a 150 rpm shaker. A sampling at predetermined intervals (added the comparable volume of release medium after each removal) and the corresponding drug release of SF and CDs were evaluated by exploiting the UV/vis. All measurements were performed at least three times.

Blood compatibility of CDs, US NPs, and USC NPs were evaluated through a hemolysis test. In brief, mice blood was gathered in an anticoagulative tube. After being washed with PBS three times by centrifugation (2500 rpm, 5 min), blood was fifty times diluted with PBS and divided into five groups. Deionized water (positive control), PBS (negative control), CDs (40 μg/mL), US NPs, and USC NPs at an equivalent concentration of CDs were added into blood tubes, respectively. Samples were standing for 3 h at 37 °C, the hemolysis study results were recorded by phone camera, and the absorbance at 540 nm was measured by a TECAN Infinite F200 Microplate Reader.

There are three types of cell lines involved in this study. Human hepatocellular carcinomas (HepG2 cells) and human hepatocellular carcinoma SMMC-7721 cells were cultured in MEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). L02 cells were cultured in an RPMI 1640 medium containing 10 % (v/v) FBS. All cells were cultured at 37 °C, a 5 % CO2 atmosphere.

The cell imaging experiment was employed to evaluate the cellular uptake of CDs and USC NPs. Specifically, the cells were incubated on a 12-well plate (3 × 105 per well) overnight until adherent. The medium was aspirated and then perform a thorough washing with PBS before being incubated with CDs solution (20 μg/mL in DMEM) and USC NPs for 4 h, respectively. The cells were then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Finally, the CLSM images of the cells were taken. Pearson’s correlation coefficient was calculated by Image J 1.8.0 to quantify the degree of colocalization between fluorophores.

The cytotoxicity and in vitro chemotherapeutic effects of various formulations upon the dark condition and laser irradiation were further determined by MTT assay. Briefly, cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (100 µL, 1 × 105 per well). After overnight incubation, replaced the previous medium with a 100 µL fresh medium containing different drugs at a certain gradient concentration for 24 h. Then, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS several times before being cultured with MTT (0.5 mg/mL, 100 μL) for an additional 4 h. Afterward, 100 µL of DMSO was added to each plate to solubilize the formazan crystals in a shaker (100 rpm, 10 min), and measure the absorbance at 490 nm by a TECAN Infinite F200 Microplate Reader.

The cell viability (%) was detected by comparing the absorbance of the sampling to control wells containing only a cell culture medium. The combination index (CI) was determined based on an established process from the literature [22,28], the CI analysis was calculated according to Eq (5):
(5)
CI =
C
A , X
IC
X , A
+
C
B , X
IC
X , B

Where CA,x, and CB,x are described as the concentrations of agents A and B, respectively, when the combined therapeutic efficiency achieved x% drug effect. ICx, A and ICx, B refer to single-dose concentrations when utilized alone to achieve the same therapeutic effect of the drug. There existed a synergism if the CI was<1.

DCFH-DA was introduced for the detection of intracellular ROS. In concise, HepG2 cells were seeded in a 12-well plate (3 × 105 cells per mL) and allowed to adhere all night. Cells were firstly incubated with DCFH-DA (10 μM, dispersed in DMEM medium) for 20 mins. After washing with PBS three times, samples were then separated into four groups: (1) The medium only; (2) The group incubated with DCFH-DA only; (3) The experimental group that was initially cultured with CDs for 4 h and bathed twice with PBS, then added 1 uL of DCFH-DA before exposing them to 638 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2) for 5 min; (4) The group incubated with USC NPs for 4 h and irradiated with 638 nm (1 W/cm2) for 5 min; All samples were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde before imaging with a confocal laser fluorescence microscope (λex = 488 nm, λem = 508–550 nm).

Mouse-derived H22 cells were utilized to erect a liver carcinoma model. Female ICR mice (6–8 weeks) were purchased from Wu Experimental Animals. All these experiments were performed following the foundations of responsibility and the use of laboratory animals. The tumor-bearing mice were intraperitoneally injected with different formulations including NPs and their counterparts. At pre-determined time intervals, the mice were anesthetized and scanned using the In Vivo Imaging System Caliper IVLS Lumina II (PerkinElmer, USA). For resected organ imaging, the animals were euthanized, and then the tumors or organs were excised and imaged (λex = 638 nm).

The H22 cells (1 × 106) in 0.9 % NaCl solution were injected subcutaneously into the right flank of each ICR mice. The tumor volume was calculated using the following formula (6):
(6)
V
( m
m
3
)
= L e n g t h × W i d t
h

/ 2

When the tumor volume reached about 60 mm3, the 35 female H22 tumor-bearing mice were randomized and divided into seven groups and received the different treatments. The tumor diameter and mice's body weight were recorded daily.
All data are shown as mean ± s.d., and differences with P < 0.05 were considered significant. Statistical differences were defined as *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

Firstly, the successful fabrication of CDs was detected by TEM. As displayed in Fig. 2
A, the CDs were observed to be well-dispersed spherical with an average particle size of 4.21 nm. Upon a 485 nm ultraviolet light excitation, the FL intensity of the emission light reached the strongest, implying the resultant CDs can emit intense red light (Fig. 2 B). Afterward, FTIR and XPS were performed to study their chemical composition and surface conditions. As depicted in Fig. 2 C, CDs displayed obvious peaks at around 1390, 1550, 3420, 3130, and 1840 cm−1, assigning to the CN, CN, CO, –OH, NH, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the existence of carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH) and amino groups (–NH2) groups [6,29]. The XPS spectrum appears to significantly explain the chemical compositions of CDs. The survey spectra demonstrated three peaks of C 1s (284.8 eV), N 1s (396.65 eV), and O 1s (528.2 eV) [30] (Fig. 2 D-G). Therefore, the surface composition of CDs was consistent with FT-IR analysis.

Among all the approaches engaged in the construction of nanomedicine, self-assembly is particularly attractive in biomedicine and nanotechnology [31–33]. Hereabouts, in this paper, NPs with synergistic antitumor effects were manufactured and optimized based on a facile and ultra-high efficiency self-assembly method. To realize the favorable therapeutic formulation of US NPs, disparate mass proportions of UA to SF (20:1, 10:1, 5:1 to 1:1, and 1:5, 1:10 to 1:20) were initially inspected ( Fig. S2 ). As exhibited in Fig. S3 , the Tyndall effect indicated that when the mass ratio of UA to SF was 1:1, the SF solution could completely dissolve UA to form a uniform solution. Significantly, both TEM (Fig. 3
A) and SEM (Fig. 3 B) images illustrated a narrow spherical with uniform size distribution. As well, the US NPs were determined to be 161 nm in size (Fig. S4
A). Thus, the favorable US NPs after optimization were utilized for subsequent use. Similarly, the size of USC NPs could be modulated and varied over a wide particle size range by adjusting the mass ratios (9:1, 6:1, 3:1 to 1:1, and 1:3, 1:6 to 1:9) of US NPs to CDs. Particularly, the USC NPs determined approximately 125.6 nm in size (Fig. S4 B) with the optimal mass ratio of US NPs to CDs of 1:3, which could result in an obvious EPR effect in case the prepared was injected into the blood. What is more, the high EE of all three mixtures was obtained with such a proportion ( Table S1 ). Approximately 83.1 % of UA, 6.0 % of CDs, and 4.7 % of SF were integrated into the final assembled NPs. At the optimal ratio, the zeta potential of NPs was almost −20 mV. Particularly, the NPs with negatively charged were reported to be susceptible to the reticular endothelial system (RES) reduction or slow turbidity, thereby contributing to prolonged blood circulation [34–36]. Hence, NPs with an optimal mass portion (1:3) of US NPs to CDs were picked for subsequent research. Also, these NPs remained in the monodispersed spherical despite being in dry circumstances, as exhibited in both TEM (Fig. 3 C) and SEM (Fig. 3 D) images, indicating the co-assembled NPs with admirable colloidal stability.

The colloid stability is inestimably dependent on the ratio of US NPs to CDs. If the ratio reaches above 1:9, as measured by DLS, the diameter of co-assembled NPs increased obviously and is distributed in the range of 200–500 nm (Fig. S2 A). Changeable, the surface charge of NPs altered from −9.51 mV to −30.6 mV by adjusting the mass ratio of US NPs to CDs from 9:1 to 1:9 (Fig. S2 D). This inspection demonstrated the intense electrostatic copulation between US NPs and CDs. The increment in size as the proportion of US NPs to CDs adjusted from 1:1 to 1:9 was ascribed to the charge neutralization at the ratio of 1:9, causing the aggregation of USC NPs. To further confirm the actuality of electrostatic interaction, the actions of differing affixing supplies of NaCl to the turbidity of USC NPs were investigated [37,38]. The disassembly of some NPs may occur due to increased ionic concentration (reflected by decreased turbidity of NPs) ( Fig. S5 ). This hints that electrostatic interactions advance the formation of NPs. All the mentioned results above infer that the synthesis was partly because of electrostatic interactions between these substances.

UV's spectra (Fig. 3 E) disclosed the as-synthesized NPs acquire typical absorption peaks from both SF (266 nm) and CDs (306 nm). This signifies that the final assemblies were indeed assembled from these species, in which they connected and acted fashioned into assemblies on the nanoscale [27,39]. The Soret band of CDs was appreciably broad and red-shifted, consecutively while the captivation band of SF also appeared to a red-shift, implying that the pyrrole group of CDs was able to interact with the aromatic ring of SF via π-π conjugate and hydrophobic [40,41]. When SDS, 0.2 % w/v was annexed to the final solution, the alteration in UV-absorption ( Fig. S6 ), as a signal of variation in the architecture of the NPs, was probably ascribed to the additional hydrophobic interactions by SDS [42]. Importantly, the presence of fluorescent signals in NPs (Fig. 3 F) indicated the successful assembly of CDs. However, compared with CDs alone, the fluorescence intensity of NPs was seriously diminished, implying the migration of excitons within stacked CDs or US NPs [43]. Taken together, hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking may be one of the primary power forces for the construction of assemblies.

To demonstrate potential applications in bioimaging and cancer therapy, the photothermal properties of CDs (QY = 10.07 %) and USC NPs (16.32 %) were thoroughly evaluated. First, the laser power-dependent temperature variations of CDs (100 μg/mL) and USC NPs at an equivalent concentration of CDs under 638 nm laser irradiation were measured. As displayed in Fig. 4 B, the temperature of CDs dispersion increases quickly with the enhancement of laser power density from 0.13 to 2.53 W/cm2. When the power achieved 2.53 W/cm2 and irradiated for 10 min, the temperatures of CDs and USC NPs achieved a maximum temperature of about 84 °C and 96.5 °C, respectively (Fig. 4 C), which indicated the enhanced photothermal conversion properties of NPs than free CDs. The reason for this may be that the CDs absorbed in the surface of NPs resulted in a condensed concentration as well as improved photostability over that of free CDs [44,45]. Based on previous studies, that carcinoma cells could be destroyed efficiently at a temperature higher than 50 °C within a few minutes [46], 1 W/cm2 could be chosen as the optimal laser power for cancer phototherapy. Second, the temperature change of free CDs and USC NPs with various concentrations (0–200 μg/mL) was measured. As revealed in Fig. 4 D, under the continuous 638 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2), the Δtemperatures (the value of temperature rise) of CDs dispersion were found to elevate from 12.7 °C (25 μg/mL) to 47.7 °C, (100 μg/mL), and the temperature of USC NPs (an equivalent CDs concentration of 100 μg/mL) irradiated under the same conditions increased from 12.3 °C to 64 °C (Fig. 4 E). The corresponding infrared imaging of CDs (Left) and USC NPs (Right) at different concentrations was shown in Fig. 4 A. In contrast, the temperature of pure water only increased by 2.5 °C. These results manifested that both CDs and USC NPs can quickly and efficiently convert the absorbed laser energy into heat. Third, to calculate the photothermal conversion efficiency (η), the temperature changes of free CDs dispersion (200 μg/mL) as a function of time was recorded (insert of Fig. 4 E), by using the fitting parameters of the cooling stage in Fig. 4 H, η of the CDs was calculated to be 35.8 % (for more details, see the experimental section); Moreover, the η of USC NPs under the same condition was calculated to be 48 % (Fig. 4 I). All of these confirmed the CDs and USC NPs have strong photothermal performance. Subsequently, the ROS generation detection study was necessary to reveal the therapeutic success rate following excellent PTT efficacy. In this experiment, DPBF was selected as a probe to investigate the ROS generation capability of the prepared CDs and USC NPs following UV irradiation. DPBF is a common ROS quencher, which reacts irreversibly with ROS produced by PSs resulting in a decrease in absorption of the DPBF at 415 nm [47]. The CDs and USC NPs combined with DPBF exhibited a prominent absorption peak at 415 nm corresponding to the DPBF (Fig. 4 F). However, significant degradation of DPBF was observed after 638 nm laser irradiation for 10 min, which demonstrated a high level of ROS generation. Overall, the optimum PTT efficacy and ROS generation shown here provided experimental evidence of the potential of the CDs and USC NPs for biomedical phototherapeutic applications. Additionally, based on the schematic diagram of CDs for phototherapy (Fig. 4 G), we may speculate that CDs can achieve non-invasive treatment of various cancers, achieving precise treatment and reducing side effects.

These associate forces, including hydrophobic, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interaction, also assured satisfactory storage stability of the self-assembled NPs in an aqueous state. The results showed that neither the size (Fig. 5
A) nor the zeta potential (Fig. 5 B) of the NPs changed significantly during storage for up to 5 days. The particle sizes of US NPs (UA/SF = 1:1) and USC NPs (US NPs/CDs = 1:3) increased to 169.2 nm and 179.5 nm, respectively, with only a slight increase compared to the initial. Furthermore, the USC NPs even maintain excellent colloidal stability in both cell culture mediums (RPMI-1640 and DMEM) containing 10 % FBS. Their particle diameter advances to roughly 200 nm with a slightly larger size distribution as compared to the initial one (Fig. 5 C).

The tumor environment has been reported to be weakly acidic [5,48]. Hence, we investigated the in vitro assembly and disassembly of NPs under differing pH conditions. Here, a pH = 5.5 PBS solution mimics the inherently acidic biosphere of the tumor area, whereas the intravascular physiological environment was simulated by PBS solution at pH 7.4. Moreover, hardly any change was observed in the size and surface charge of NPs stationed in various pH conditions ( Fig. S7 ). Both the DLS measurement (Fig. S7
A) and TEM observation ( Fig. S8 ) revealed that the size distribution of NPs was broader at pH 5.5, demonstrating the disintegration of some NPs. The slight decrease in the zeta potential of NPs (Fig. S7 B) was mainly attributed to the protonation of amino groups in US NPs under acidic conditions [49,50].

The in vitro release portrait of NPs was monitored under various pH surroundings (pH = 5.5 and pH = 7.4). Likewise, the drug release characteristics of free SF and CDs have hardly any differences under diverse pH environments. All CDs were completely released within 8 h ( Fig. S9 ). On the contrary, the NPs’ release rate turned slower in the above situations. In the initial 8 h, approximately 22 % of CDs and 25 % of SF were released from NPs in a PBS solution of pH 7.4 (Fig. 5 D). Under the acidic (pH = 5.5) solution, the release rate of SF got a little faster. On the contrary, the release rate of CDs becomes slower. More than 40 % of SF and nearly 26 % of CDs were released from NPs at the pH of 5.5 within 48 h, which might be beneficial for the efficacy of chemotherapy and PDT. This result indicated the NPs with acid-responsive capacity. The comparably more rapid release rate of SF might arise from the fact that the amino group of SF was more willingly protonated at acidic conditions, which enhances its solubility and advances its dispersion in the aqueous solution [51]. Likewise, the more sluggish release of CDs was ascribed to its weakened solubility associative with the carboxyl (–COOH) groups in acidic environments. This phenomenon could be attributed to that the carboxyl groups are hydrophobic under acidic conditions and hydrophilic under alkaline conditions [52]. According to our previous study [23], the self-assembly of UA and SF mainly depends on hydrophobic interaction. The surface of USC NPs contained a large number of carboxyl groups, and their hydrophilicity gradually increased as the pH increased. However, when the USC NPs situated in an alkaline environment, the USC NPs was hydrophilic because relatively few carboxyl groups were present in the USC NPs and were unable to reach a hydrophobic state under acidic conditions.

Based on the encouraging attributes of USC NPs, their feasibilities in fluorescence (FL) imaging and synergistic treatment were investigated at the cellular level. Cellular internalization of drugs is known to play a crucial role in PDT, and chemotherapeutic efficiency. Therefore, the uptake of CDs incubated at different times (1 h, 2 h, 4 h, and 8 h) by HepG2 cells was further studied ( Fig. S10
A). A small fraction of CDs were initially adsorbed to the surface of cells within 1 h of incubation, most of the CDs reached the intracellular environment following 4 h incubation and were saturated during this period. More importantly, the Pearson's correlation ( Fig. S10
B) of CDs first increased and then decreased with the prolongation of incubation time, reaching the maximum value after 4 h incubation, which was chosen for subsequent use. In addition, CDs (20 ug/mL) can specifically stain the nucleolus of the treated cells into blue fluorescence and increase with the prolongation of incubation time within 4 h, indicating the CDs synthesized with highly universal and specific nucleolar targeting capacity. The favorable cellular internalization of CDs may be related to the previously reported mechanism that negatively charged tend to bind with the positive charges on the cell membrane, which would further initiate cellular internalization in a relatively slow “adsorbent endocytosis” pathway [53].

Considering the excellent photostability of CDs, the channel was chosen for subsequent use. Then, the feasibility of FL imaging of CDs and USC NPs towards three types of cell lines (HepG2 cells, SMMC 7721 cells, and L02 cells) further experimented with CLSM. As revealed in Fig. 6 A, the HepG2 cells and SMMC 7721 with bright emission can be distinguished easily as compared to the L02 cell, thus verifying efficient cell penetration. Correspondingly, the blue colocalization fluorescence and Pearson's correlation coefficient of CDs and USC NPs in HepG2 cells and SMMC 7721 cells were higher than those in L02 cells (Fig. 6 B), indicating the excellent FL characteristics of CDs and USC NPs, which endowed them a candidate material for fluorescence imaging agents.

For in vitro chemotherapy, the cell viability of normal cells (L02 cells) and carcinoma cells (HepG2 cells) after being incubated with different drugs was detected by MTT assays. The cytotoxicity of the NPs and their counterparts were investigated in these two cell lines, as revealed in Fig. 7 A and B, from which we can notice that the L02 cells maintained a survival rate above 80 % despite that the NPs reaching a concentration of 10 μg/mL. On the contrary, as the drug concentration increases, the survival rate of HepG2 cells decreased rapidly. After being treated with 40 μg/mL of USC NPs, only 15.51 % of HepG2 cells survived, verifying the killing effect of NPs on cancer cells was higher than that of normal cells. The NPs upon laser irradiation realized the most acceptable efficiency in killing tumor cells. The CI calculated<1 (CI = 0.866) implied a synergism in combining phototherapy (CDs) and chemotherapy (US NPs). Particularly, no significant cytotoxicity was observed for CDs solution without laser irradiation in HepG2 cells. In contrast, the cell viability decreased gradually with the increase of CDs concentration under laser irradiation. Besides, the antitumor efficiency of UA and SF was not affected by the introduction of CDs, as seen from the similar dark cytotoxicity of antitumor drugs and NPs. Further, the apoptosis experiments (see Fig. 7 E) showed that the USC NPs and their counterparts could induce apoptosis of HepG2 cells to vary degrees compared with medium only, the apoptosis rates of UA, SF, CDs with laser irradiation, US NPs, and USC NPs with laser irradiation were 13.57 %, 21.19 %, 28.09 %, 40.6 %, and 83.53 %, respectively. Further, compared with the control group, the cells in the experimental group showed different degrees of swelling, rounding and brightening, reduced number, uneven cytoplasm, and some cells showed shrinkage or even blurred boundaries (Fig. 7 C), indicating experimental group could kill HepG2 cells to varying degrees, and the combination group had the best effect, namely, the number of cells in the USC NPs with laser irradiation group was significantly reduced. Reports on the important role of ROS in CD-induced apoptosis encouraged us to further investigate the intracellular ROS levels using the ROS probe (DCFH-DA) [54]. The brighter green fluorescence observed using a confocal microscope (Fig. 7 D) and the enhanced ROS generation through quantitative analysis by Pearson’s correlation coefficient confirmed the superior anticancer activities of USC NPs. The result was consistent with the results of apoptosis.

Inspired by the exceptional synergistic therapeutic efficacy of USC NPs at the cellular level, their fluorescence imaging and anti-tumor actions were further studied in vivo. First of all, the blood biocompatibility of US NPs, CDs, and USC NPs was determined by a hemolysis test to evaluate their safety in anti-tumor applications. As shown in Fig. S11 , compared with the positive control group, no significant color of red blood cells was observed upon a high concentration of CDs (40 μg/mL), US NPs, and USC NPs at an equivalent concentration of CDs, demonstrating their high hemocompatibility. Then, the In vivo fluorescence was manipulated after the injection of Cy 5.5, CDs, and USC NPs to determine their accumulation. As shown in Fig. 8
A, the fluorescence of the Cy5.5 and CDs-treated group were randomly distributed all over the body. However, the apparent tumor accumulation of the NPs administrated gradually increased and a peak tumor signal was observed around 4 h, which was further verified by fluorescence quantification (Fig. 8 D). Particularly, only the groups injected with USC NPs still had accumulated drugs in the tumor sites at 24 h post-administration as compared to those of free Cy5.5 and CDs. Illustrating the elaborately designed NPs with favorable EPR effects as well as better blood circulation time. The ex vivo imaging results further confirmed the higher drug accumulation of NPs than that of Cy5.5 and free CDs solution in the tumor site (Fig. 8 B). Another distinct divergence between these three groups held that the Cy5.5 and free CDs were freely distributed to the whole body, however, a large number of NPs was inclined to aggregate in the liver, which might be due to the differences in the size of CDs and the disparate of NPs in position state (Fig. 8 B and 8C), which further suggest the metabolic pathway of NPs is through the liver.

Based on the results of biological distribution, the photothermal properties of CDs and USC NPs were further investigated by an infrared thermography camera at the optimal time point (4 h) after injection, the corresponding temperature variation curve was recorded. Due to strong absorption and efficient photothermal conversion ability, the tumor temperature of mice injected with CDs and USC NPs under 638 nm laser irradiation for 5 min rapidly elevated to around 46.4 °C and 52.4 °C, respectively, which was high enough to effectively ablate tumors (Fig. 9
A). However, the limited temperature at tumor sites of PBS injected groups were 39.9. Furthermore, the tumor temperature of mice in the USC NPs treated group was noted to be elevated more quickly than that of the PBS and free CDs treated group (Fig. 9 E), validating the improved accumulate capacity and PTT efficiency of NPs in vivo attributed to their relatively large diameter.

Motivated by the excellent tumor homing capacity, the synergistic therapeutic efficiency of NPs was performed. Tumor-bearing female mice xenografted with H22 tumors were randomly divided into seven groups and given the following different treatments: 1) Blank group (PBS + laser), 2) Chemodynamic therapy group (free UA), 3) Chemotherapy group (free SF), 4) PTT + PDT group (CDs + laser), 5) Chemotherapy group (US NPs), 6) Chemotherapy group (USC NPs without laser irradiation), 7) Chemotherapy + PTT + PDT group (USC NPs + laser). All materials were injected via tail vein at the concentration of 15 mg kg−1 of UA; 15 mg kg−1 of SF; 45 mg kg−1 of CDs; 15 mg kg−1 of UA + 15 mg kg−1 of SF and 15 mg kg−1 of UA + 15 mg kg−1 of SF + 45 mg kg−1 of CDs every other day. After the appliance of seven group treatments, the tumor volume and body weight of mice were recorded daily. As revealed in Fig. 9 B, mice's body weight in the chemotherapy + PTT + PDT group decreased slightly for the initial observation period, then gradually went back to the normal level in the latter days. Fig. 9 D presented the related tumor volume alteration trends of diverse groups. In comparison with the blank group, a certain impact on tumor expansion was observed in the chemotherapy group as well as the PDT + chemotherapy group, which demonstrated these groups possess some anti-tumor efficiency. In the meantime, the mice of final NPs displayed appreciable tumor reticence due to their chemotherapy effect. After exposure to 638 nm laser irradiation, mice in the PDT + PTT group had noteworthy inhibitory efficiency on tumor growth, and the average relative tumor volume was reduced, implying the combination of PDT and PTT had a considerable antitumor effect. As expected, the tumor tissue resected after 21 days of administration further verified that the chemotherapy + PDT + PTT group had the maximal tumor inhibition rate and the tumor clearance rate was as high as 85 % (Fig. 9 C). All these results confirmed a significant reduction in tumor volume and the highest efficacy of triple therapy.

In summary, we developed a versatile anti-cancer theranostic platform through the green, facile and efficient construction of UA, SF, and CDs in this paper, which allowed all cancerous cell-targeting, acid-responsive, cell imaging, synergistic therapy, nucleolar targeting, and phototherapy function. Compared with the reported CDs for PTT whose efficacy was limited by the ineffective diagnosis and incapable of overseeing the follow-up therapeutic effect [55,56], the PTT agent in this work enabled accurate identification of the tumor’s size and location. Meanwhile, it confers a real-time monitor of the therapeutic effect in the region of interest for the plan of better intervention. The key improvements in this work were that the size of USC NPs was significantly increased compared to CDs alone, facilitating their accumulation at tumor sites due to the EPR effects, further, it enabled a synergistic anti-tumor effect in vivo and eventually eradicated the tumor during one treatment cycle. Therefore, these NPs might be a promising candidates for the chemo-phototherapy triple therapy against tumors in their preclinical studies. Importantly, the CDs PSs with high ROS generation performance and nucleolus targeting capacity, provide a more effective and ideal site for cancer therapy. All in all, our NPs can give an entire extension to the effect of “killing three birds with one stone”. The rationale design of such a nanoplatform might open an avenue and give the inspiration to construct novel theranostics and hold promise for the fabrication of advanced minimalist chemo-photo therapeutic. Moreover, this strategy can be further combined with other immunomodulators to further explore the mechanism of immunosuppression.
